tool.name: Dorado demultiplexer
tool.command:
mkdir -p output
&& ln -s '$reads' reads
&& dorado demux --output-dir output --threads "\${GALAXY_SLOTS}" --emit-summary #if $classify.classify_mode == 'classify' --kit-name '${classify.kit_name}' #if $classify.barcode_both_ends --barcode-both-ends #end if #else --no-classify #end if #if $sample_sheet --sample-sheet '${sample_sheet}' #end if #if $no_trim --no-trim #end if reads

param.name: reads
param.label: Basecalled reads (BAM)
param.help: A BAM file produced by the Dorado basecaller. Raw POD5 data is not accepted here — you must basecall it first with the Dorado basecalling tool on Galaxy. See the Help section below for full details.

param.name: classify_mode
param.label: Barcode classification mode
param.option: Classify reads using a barcode kit

param.label: Barcode kit
param.help: The barcode kit used during library preparation. If you don't know which kit was used, see the "Identifying the barcode kit" section in the Help below.

param.label: Require barcodes on both ends
param.help: For double-ended kits, require barcodes on both ends of a read for classification. Reads with a barcode on only one end will be classified as unclassified.

param.label: Sample sheet
param.help: Restrict or alias barcode classifications using a sample sheet file.

param.label: Preserve barcodes (no trimming)
param.help: Do not trim barcode sequences from reads. Required if the input reads are aligned and you wish to preserve the alignment information in the output.

tool.help:
Demultiplex barcoded Oxford Nanopore reads into per-barcode output files using Oxford Nanopore's open source `Dorado <https://github.com/nanoporetech/dorado/>`__ basecaller.
This tool classifies basecalled reads by their barcode sequence and writes them into individual BAM files — one per detected barcode — plus an ``unclassified.bam`` for reads that could not be assigned.
Input requirements -------------------
The input to this tool **must be a BAM file** produced by the Dorado basecaller. Raw POD5 data is **not** accepted here — POD5 contains raw electrical signal which has to be basecalled into nucleotide sequences before demultiplexing can take place.
If you have POD5 data, the workflow is:
1. Run the **Dorado basecalling** tool on Galaxy to convert your POD5 file(s) into a basecalled BAM file. 2. Pass that BAM file into this tool to split it by barcode.
The basecaller's ``--kit-name`` option only *tags* reads with their barcode classification — it does **not** split them into separate files. This tool performs the actual split. You can therefore choose either of two patterns:
- Set ``--kit-name`` during basecalling (reads are pre-classified) and then run this tool in **Use existing barcode annotations** mode, or - Skip ``--kit-name`` during basecalling and run this tool in **Classify reads using a barcode kit** mode to classify and split in one step.
Identifying the barcode kit ----------------------------
If you don't know which barcode kit was used to prepare your library, try the following, in order of reliability:
1. **Ask your sequencing facility or the person who prepared the library** — the library preparation kit is recorded at the bench and is the most reliable source. 2. **Inspect the POD5 metadata** if you still have the raw data. From a terminal::
pip install pod5 pod5 inspect read your_file.pod5 <read_id>
Look for ``sequencing_kit`` or ``barcoding_kits`` in the ``context_tags`` field of the printed run info. 3. **Inspect the BAM header** if the file was basecalled with ``--kit-name`` — the kit name is embedded in the ``@RG`` read group identifiers::
samtools view -H your.bam | grep '@RG'
Look for a segment like ``..._SQK-RBK114-96_barcode01`` in the ``ID`` field; the kit name is the part immediately before ``_barcode``.
Common kit-name prefixes:
- ``SQK-RBK*``, ``SQK-NBD*``, ``SQK-PCB*``, ``SQK-16S*``, ``SQK-MLK*`` — barcoded (multiplexed) kits - ``EXP-NBD*``, ``EXP-PBC*``, ``TWIST-*`` — barcode expansion kits, used alongside an ``SQK-LSK*`` ligation kit - ``SQK-LSK*``, ``SQK-RAD*``, ``SQK-ULK*`` — **single-sample** ligation / rapid kits (not barcoded; this tool is not applicable)
Barcode classification modes -----------------------------
**Classify reads using a barcode kit**: Re-classify reads by scanning for the barcode sequences belonging to the specified kit. Use this when your reads were not classified during basecalling, or when you want to re-classify with a different kit.
Note: if the reads were previously trimmed (the default during basecalling), barcode flanking regions may have been removed, which can reduce classification accuracy. To avoid this, set adapter/primer trimming to ``None`` in the Dorado basecalling tool, then demultiplex as a separate step.
**Use existing barcode annotations**: Skip re-classification and split reads using the barcode information already embedded in the reads (the ``BC`` BAM tag). Use this when reads were already classified during basecalling.
Barcode trimming -----------------
By default, barcode sequences are trimmed from the output reads. Enable **Preserve barcodes** (``--no-trim``) to retain them. This is required when the input reads carry alignment information that you want to preserve in the output files.
Sample sheet ------------
A sample sheet can be used to restrict demultiplexing to a subset of barcodes, or to apply alias names to barcodes in the output filenames.
